sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current player position
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] == 24:
        print(f"Player: boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Check ascii around player area
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\nRows 10-20, cols 18-30:")
for r in range(10, 21):
    print(f"  {r}: {ascii_lines[r][18:30]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:
The player is at (8,20)-(12,24). Row 13 is all black (BBBBBBBBBBBB). The white cells are at rows 14-18, cols 20-24.
So the player can't move DOWN from (8,20)-(12,24) because row 13 is black.
Hmm, but the white corridor is at rows 14-18, cols 20-24, which is directly below the player. Maybe the player needs to move to a different position to access this corridor.
Let me check the white corridor at (14,20)-(18,24). It's a 5x5 white area. The player is a 5x5 blue block. Maybe the player can "fall" into this corridor if there's a gap.
Actually, I notice that row 13 is all black, but the white corridor starts at row 14. Maybe the player needs to move to a position where there's a gap in row 13.
Let me check the ascii more carefully. The white corridor is at rows 14-18, cols 20-24. The cells at row 13, cols 20-24 are black.
Wait, I think I need to reconsider the movement mechanics. The player moves in discrete jumps of 6 columns/rows. When the player moves DOWN, it jumps from row 8-12 to row 14-18 (a jump of 6 rows).
But the player can only move DOWN if the cells at rows 14-18, cols 20-24 are white (which they are). The issue is that row 13 is black, which blocks the movement.
Actually, I think the player doesn't need a gap in row 13. The player might be able to "jump" over row 13 to the white corridor at rows 14-18.
Let me try DOWN again from (8,20)-(12,24).
